fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build that proxy accelerator thing we've been talking about. Basically it sits in front of our app server and handles all the boring stuff so the app doesn't have to. The big things are: forwarding requests to the upstream without mangling them, some kind of response reuse so the origin isn't hammered with the same request over and over, fast file delivery where the app just points at a file path and the proxy handles sending it (there was a nasty bug with compressed files last time, make sure that's handled properly — similar to how we fixed the encoding issue in the asset pipeline), and we need a log line for every request so ops can debug things. There's also a config system that reads from the environment and args, and an in-memory store for the cache backend. Structure this properly please — last time someone built something like this it ended up as one giant file and nobody could maintain it. Split things out by responsibility. The whole thing needs to be driven from stdin/stdout for the test harness but keep that adapter layer separate from the actual logic. Check the existing test scaffolding for the exact input/output shapes — the cases in the rcb_tests folder should tell you what you need. Make sure edge cases like oversized payloads, missing upstream command, and private/no-store directives are handled explicitly.

trigger_keywords: when should a response be stored
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A response is storable only when two conditions are simultaneously true: (1) the Cache-Control directives explicitly mark it as shareable — meaning `public` must be present — AND (2) a positive freshness lifetime is granted via a `max-age` value greater than zero. Any response marked `private`, any response missing the `public` directive, any response with `no-store` or `no-cache`, and any response with `max-age=0` or no `max-age` at all must NOT be stored. Additionally, only HTTP 200 OK responses are stored; informational (1xx), redirect (3xx), not-modified (304), and error (4xx/5xx) statuses are never stored. Bodies exceeding the configured size limit are also never stored.

trigger_keywords: how is the cache key derived
which requests are considered equivalent for caching
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The cache key is composed of three parts: the HTTP method, the URL path, and the complete set of query parameters. Query parameters are treated as order-independent — `?a=1&b=2` and `?b=2&a=1` must produce the same key. When a `Vary` header is present in the origin response, the values of exactly the named request headers are also incorporated into the key, so different values for those headers produce distinct cache entries. Headers not named in `Vary` are irrelevant to the key. Only GET requests are eligible for caching; POST and other non-GET methods must always bypass the cache (disposition: `bypass`).

When X-Sendfile is active and the origin response includes a `Content-Encoding` header (indicating the file on disk is pre-compressed), the proxy must still set `Content-Length` to the true on-disk byte size of the file rather than inheriting the origin's value (which may be zero or wrong). This prevents truncated downloads. The `Content-Type` is inferred by sniffing the file, `Content-Length` is set to the real file size, the delegation header (`X-Sendfile`) is stripped from the client-visible response, and any body the origin wrote is discarded. When the feature is disabled, the `X-Sendfile` header passes through to the client unchanged.

trigger_keywords: what fields does the access log record
what does the log line contain
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each log record must capture the following fields in order: `path` (without query string), `method`, `status`, `remote_addr` (taken from the `X-Forwarded-For` request header when present, otherwise the direct client address), `user_agent`, `req_content_type`, `resp_content_type`, `req_content_length` (byte length of the request body), `resp_content_length` (bytes written in the response body), `query` (raw query string), and `cache` (the X-Cache disposition reported by the downstream handler, e.g. miss/hit/bypass). Timing fields must NOT be included.

trigger_keywords: what happens when upstream command is missing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Configuration is loaded from process arguments and environment variables. The first positional argument after the program name is the upstream command to run; if it is absent, loading must fail with a normalized error: `error=missing_upstream_command`. All other settings have defaults and can be overridden by environment variables; each variable may be provided either bare (e.g. `PORT`) or with a fixed namespace prefix (e.g. `THRUSTER_PORT`), and the prefixed form takes precedence over the bare form. Default values: `target_port=3000`, `cache_size=67108864`, `max_request_body=0`, `x_sendfile_enabled=true`, `http_read_timeout_seconds=30`, `acme_directory=https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory`, `log_level=info`.

trigger_keywords: how does the memcache store work
what happens when an item exceeds capacity
per-item size limit vs total capacity
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The in-memory store holds byte payloads under numeric keys with per-entry TTL expiry, a total capacity limit, and a per-item size limit. Storing a key replaces any prior payload for that key. Retrieving a key returns its payload when present and unexpired. An item whose size exceeds EITHER the total capacity OR the per-item size limit is rejected and must never become retrievable — a subsequent `get` must return `found=false` with `value_len=0`. The `set` operation always reports the value length even for oversized items that are rejected. Expired items are also treated as not found.

trigger_keywords: how should the codebase be structured
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must be organized as a multi-file tree with one cohesive unit per responsibility. Required separation: routing/proxy forwarding, caching policy, file delivery (sendfile), structured logging, configuration loading, the in-memory cache store, and the stdin/stdout JSON adapter must each live in distinct modules or files. A single monolithic file is explicitly prohibited. The JSON adapter that reads from stdin and writes to stdout must be decoupled from the core request-handling components — the adapter alone translates JSON test commands into calls to the core and renders results.

trigger_keywords: what cache disposition values are possible
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are exactly three cache disposition values: `miss` — the request reached the origin and the response was freshly generated (it may or may not have been stored, but nothing was replayed); `hit` — a previously stored response was replayed and the origin was not contacted; `bypass` — the request was deliberately not eligible for caching (e.g. POST or non-GET method) and went straight to the origin without cache lookup or storage. These values are reported in the `X-Cache` response header and captured by the logging middleware.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'proxy', 'cache', 'sendfile', 'logging', 'memcache', 'config'. Each value maps to one feature.

show me feature1 proxy routing test cases

give me the feature1_proxy_routing test file
what does feature1 proxy routing expect

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact input/output contract, and refer to the start.md PRD for architectural constraints. If you have a more specific question about routing, caching rules, sendfile behavior, logging fields, config loading, or the memcache store, ask me directly and I can clarify.

feature1_proxy_routing.json.description: A reverse proxy forwards an incoming HTTP request to a single fixed upstream origin and relays the origin's response back to the client unchanged. The request method, path and query string must be delivered to the upstream exactly as received (routing fidelity), and the upstream's status code, Content-Type header and response body must be returned verbatim to the caller. Output reports the client-visible status, content type and body, plus the method, path and query string actually observed at the upstream.

cases.expected_output:
status=200
content_type=text/plain; charset=utf-8
upstream_method=GET
upstream_path=/articles
upstream_query=page=2
body=Article listing body

status=404
content_type=application/json
upstream_method=POST
upstream_path=/submit
upstream_query=id=7
body={"error":"missing"}

feature2_1_caching.json.description: A caching layer sits in front of an origin and decides whether each response may be reused for later identical requests, based solely on the response's caching directives, status and size. A response is storable only when its directives mark it explicitly shareable AND give a positive freshness lifetime; directives that mark it private, omit a shareable marker, give a zero or absent lifetime, or explicitly forbid reuse make it non-storable. Redirect/informational/error or not-modified statuses and bodies larger than the configured limit are never stored. Each request reports a cache disposition signal: a freshly generated (and possibly stored) response, a reuse of a previously stored response, or a deliberate skip of the cache. When a stored response is reused, the original status code and any custom headers it carried are reproduced exactly. The origin numbers successive generated bodies so that a reused response is distinguishable from a regenerated one. Output, per request in the sequence, reports the request index, status code, cache disposition, any cookie header, a tracked custom header, and the body.

cases.expected_output:
request=0
status=200
x_cache=miss
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello 1
request=1
status=200
x_cache=hit
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello 1
request=2
status=200
x_cache=hit
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello 1

request=0
status=200
x_cache=bypass
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello 1
request=1
status=200
x_cache=bypass
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello 2
request=2
status=200
x_cache=bypass
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello 3

feature2_2_keying.json.description: The caching layer derives a cache key for each request so that only genuinely equivalent requests share a stored response. The key is sensitive to the request method, the URL path and the full set of query parameters, but treats query parameters as order-independent (the same parameters supplied in a different order map to the same key). Methods that are not safely cacheable bypass the cache entirely. With an always-storable origin response, output per request reports the request index, status code, cache disposition (fresh generation, reuse, or cache skip), cookie header, tracked custom header, and body, so the keying behaviour is visible through which requests reuse earlier responses.

cases.expected_output:
request=0
status=200
x_cache=miss
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello
request=1
status=200
x_cache=miss
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello
request=2
status=200
x_cache=miss
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello
request=3
status=200
x_cache=hit
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello

request=0
status=200
x_cache=miss
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello
request=1
status=200
x_cache=hit
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello
request=2
status=200
x_cache=hit
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=Hello

feature2_3_vary.json.description: When an origin response declares that it varies by one or more request header fields, the caching layer incorporates exactly those request header values into the cache key, so that clients sending different values for the named headers receive distinct stored variants while differences in unnamed headers are ignored. The origin echoes the negotiated header value into its body so each variant is observable. With multiple varying header fields, a stored variant is reused only when every named header matches. Output per request reports the request index, status code, cache disposition, cookie header, tracked custom header, and the negotiated body.

cases.expected_output:
request=0
status=200
x_cache=miss
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=application/json
request=1
status=200
x_cache=hit
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=application/json
request=2
status=200
x_cache=miss
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=text/plain
request=3
status=200
x_cache=hit
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=text/plain
request=4
status=200
x_cache=hit
set_cookie=-
extra=-
body=application/json

feature3_sendfile.json.description: An accelerated file-delivery feature lets the origin delegate sending a static file to the proxy by returning a header naming a path on disk instead of writing the file body itself. When the feature is enabled, the proxy advertises support to the origin (a request header), and on seeing the delegation header it reads the named file from disk, sends its contents as the response body (discarding any body the origin wrote), strips the delegation header, and sets the Content-Length to the real file size and the Content-Type by sniffing the file. Crucially, even when the origin marked the response with a Content-Encoding, the proxy still sets Content-Length to the true on-disk size rather than passing through the origin's (possibly zero) value, preventing truncated responses. If no delegation header is present the origin's own response (status, headers, body) is returned untouched. When the feature is disabled the proxy neither advertises support nor intercepts the delegation header, which then passes straight through. Output reports the status, Content-Type, Content-Length, Content-Encoding, the delegation header's final state, and the body.

upstream.body: this body should not be seen

cases.expected_output:
status=200
content_type=text/plain; charset=utf-8
content_length=24
content_encoding=-
x_sendfile=-
body=Sendfile body contents.

status=418
content_type=application/custom
content_length=-
content_encoding=-
x_sendfile=<passthrough>
body=This body should be seen

feature4_logging.json.description: A logging middleware wraps the request handling and emits one structured record summarizing each completed request. The record captures the request path (without query string), HTTP method, final response status code, the client address (taken from the forwarded-for header when present), the user agent, the request and response Content-Type values, the request body length and the number of response bytes written, the raw query string, and the cache disposition reported downstream. Timing-related fields are excluded. Output renders the captured fields as neutral key/value lines.

cases.expected_output:
path=/somepath
method=POST
status=201
remote_addr=192.168.1.1
user_agent=Robot/1
req_content_type=application/json
resp_content_type=text/html
req_content_length=5
resp_content_length=8
query=q=ok
cache=miss

feature5_memcache.json.description: An in-memory key/value store holds byte payloads under numeric keys with a per-entry expiry time, a total capacity and a per-item size limit. Storing a key records its payload; storing the same key again replaces the prior payload. Retrieving a key returns its current payload when present and unexpired. An item whose size exceeds the total capacity (or the per-item limit) is rejected and never becomes retrievable. Output reports, per operation, either the stored payload length or the retrieval outcome (found flag plus the payload, or the payload length for large values).

cases.expected_output:
set key=1 value_len=11
get key=1 found=true value=hello world

set key=1 value_len=10240
get key=1 found=false value_len=0

feature6_config.json.description: A configuration loader builds the runtime settings from process arguments and environment variables. The first positional argument is the upstream command to run; if it is missing, loading fails. Every tunable setting has a default that is overridden by an environment variable; a variable may be given either bare or with a fixed namespace prefix, and the prefixed form takes precedence over the bare form. A debug flag raises the log level. Output reports the resolved settings as neutral key/value lines, or a normalized error when the required upstream command is absent.

cases.expected_output:
target_port=3000
upstream_command=echo
cache_size=67108864
max_request_body=0
x_sendfile_enabled=true
http_read_timeout_seconds=30
acme_directory=https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
log_level=info
